description: Backend interview questions covering computer networks, operating systems, databases, Redis, and message queues.

questions.content: Explain the TCP/IP layered model and why network protocols are layered.
reference_points: TCP/IP is commonly described as application, transport, network, and network interface layers
Layering separates responsibilities and hides implementation details between layers
It improves maintainability because each layer can evolve behind stable interfaces
Application protocols such as HTTP run above transport protocols such as TCP or UDP
The model helps debug network issues by locating the failing layer
follow_up_directions: How does TCP/IP map to the OSI model?
Which layer is responsible for routing?

questions.content: What happens after a user enters a URL in a browser and presses Enter?
reference_points: The browser parses the URL and checks cache, HSTS, and local DNS information
DNS resolves the domain name to an IP address
The client establishes a TCP connection and may perform a TLS handshake for HTTPS
The browser sends an HTTP request and receives an HTTP response
The browser parses HTML, downloads subresources, executes scripts, and renders the page
follow_up_directions: Where can latency be reduced in this process?
How does HTTPS change the connection setup?

questions.content: Compare HTTP and HTTPS from a backend engineering perspective.
reference_points: HTTPS is HTTP over TLS and provides encryption, integrity, and server authentication
HTTP commonly uses port 80, while HTTPS commonly uses port 443
TLS introduces handshake overhead but protects sensitive data in transit
Certificates and certificate chains are required for server identity verification
Backend services should enforce HTTPS for login, payment, and private data APIs
follow_up_directions: What is the role of a CA?
How does TLS termination work behind a load balancer?

questions.content: What is the difference between HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3?
reference_points: HTTP/1.1 supports persistent connections but can suffer application-level head-of-line blocking
HTTP/2 introduces binary framing, multiplexing, and header compression over TCP
HTTP/2 still suffers from TCP-level head-of-line blocking when packet loss occurs
HTTP/3 uses QUIC over UDP and improves connection setup and stream independence
Protocol choice affects latency, connection reuse, and behavior under packet loss
follow_up_directions: Why does HTTP/2 still have head-of-line blocking?
What is QUIC connection migration?

questions.content: Explain the TCP three-way handshake and why two handshakes are not enough.
reference_points: The client sends SYN to request a connection
The server replies with SYN-ACK to acknowledge and advertise its own sequence number
The client sends ACK to confirm the server sequence number
The process synchronizes initial sequence numbers in both directions
Two handshakes cannot reliably prove that both sides can send and receive
follow_up_directions: What is SYN flood?
How do TCP sequence numbers help reliability?

questions.content: Explain TCP four-way connection termination and the purpose of TIME_WAIT.
reference_points: TCP closes each direction of a full-duplex connection independently
The active closer sends FIN and receives ACK
The peer later sends its own FIN and receives the final ACK
TIME_WAIT lets delayed packets expire and allows retransmission of the final ACK
Too many TIME_WAIT sockets can indicate connection churn or missing connection reuse
follow_up_directions: Why is TIME_WAIT usually on the active closer?
How would you reduce excessive short connections?

questions.content: Compare TCP and UDP and give backend scenarios for each.
reference_points: TCP is connection-oriented and provides reliable ordered byte streams
UDP is connectionless and has lower overhead but no built-in reliability
HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 commonly use TCP, while DNS often uses UDP
Real-time media, metrics, and some gaming scenarios can prefer UDP
QUIC adds reliability and security features on top of UDP
follow_up_directions: Why does DNS often use UDP?
Can UDP be reliable at the application layer?

questions.content: How does DNS resolution work, and what can go wrong in production?
reference_points: DNS translates domain names into IP addresses through recursive and authoritative resolvers
Clients and resolvers cache results based on TTL
Failures can come from expired records, wrong CNAME chains, resolver issues, or DNS pollution
Low TTL improves agility but increases resolver load
Production debugging should inspect local cache, resolver response, authoritative records, and network reachability
follow_up_directions: How would you migrate traffic using DNS?
What is the risk of setting TTL too high?

questions.content: What is the difference between WebSocket, HTTP polling, and Server-Sent Events?
reference_points: Polling repeatedly sends HTTP requests and is simple but inefficient for real-time updates
SSE uses one-way server-to-client streaming over HTTP
WebSocket provides full-duplex communication after an upgrade handshake
WebSocket is suitable for chat, interviews, dashboards, and collaborative tools
SSE can be simpler when only server-to-client token streaming is needed
follow_up_directions: Which one would you choose for LLM token streaming?
How would you handle WebSocket reconnection?

questions.content: What are common HTTP status codes a backend developer should know?
reference_points: 2xx means successful handling, such as 200 OK and 201 Created
3xx indicates redirection or cache-related behavior
4xx means client-side request issues such as 400, 401, 403, 404, and 409
5xx means server-side failures such as 500, 502, 503, and 504
Choosing accurate status codes improves client behavior and API observability
follow_up_directions: When would you use 409?
What is the difference between 401 and 403?

questions.content: How would you debug a backend API that occasionally times out?
reference_points: Check client timeout, gateway timeout, service timeout, and database timeout settings
Use tracing to locate slow spans across network, application, cache, and database
Inspect connection pool exhaustion and slow DNS or TLS handshakes
Check whether retries amplify traffic during partial failures
Add request ids and latency percentiles for observability
follow_up_directions: How would you set timeout budgets across services?
Why can retries make an outage worse?

questions.content: What is head-of-line blocking, and where can it appear?
reference_points: Head-of-line blocking means later work is blocked by earlier work in the same queue or stream
HTTP/1.1 can suffer when responses share one connection sequentially
HTTP/2 solves application-level multiplexing but packet loss can still block at TCP level
Message queues and database locks can also create similar blocking patterns
Mitigations include multiplexing, connection pools, sharding, and independent streams
follow_up_directions: How does QUIC reduce this issue?
Can a database lock create head-of-line blocking?

questions.content: How do load balancers distribute traffic, and what problems should backend services consider?
reference_points: Common strategies include round robin, weighted round robin, least connections, and consistent hashing
Health checks prevent routing traffic to unhealthy instances
Sticky sessions can simplify stateful apps but reduce flexibility
Backend services should be stateless or store session state externally
Load balancers must handle timeouts, retries, TLS termination, and connection reuse carefully
follow_up_directions: When would you use consistent hashing?
Why are sticky sessions risky?

questions.content: What is CORS, and why do frontend-backend applications encounter it?
reference_points: CORS is a browser security mechanism for cross-origin requests
The server must explicitly allow origins, methods, and headers
Some requests trigger a preflight OPTIONS request
CORS errors are enforced by browsers, not usually by backend HTTP clients
Overly permissive CORS settings can create security risk
follow_up_directions: What triggers a preflight request?
Why does curl not reproduce a browser CORS error?

questions.content: How would you design API timeout, retry, and circuit breaker policies between microservices?
reference_points: Timeouts should be shorter than upstream deadlines and based on latency percentiles
Retries should be bounded, jittered, and only used for idempotent operations
Circuit breakers fail fast when a dependency is unhealthy
Bulkheads prevent one dependency from exhausting all resources
Policies should be observable and tested under partial failure
follow_up_directions: How would you avoid retry storms?
What makes an API operation idempotent?

questions.content: What is the role of an operating system in backend application execution?
reference_points: The OS manages CPU, memory, files, devices, network, and security
Applications interact with hardware through system calls and OS abstractions
The kernel handles privileged operations such as memory management and I/O
Backend performance is affected by scheduling, networking, file systems, and memory pressure
Understanding OS behavior helps debug latency, resource leaks, and deployment issues
follow_up_directions: What is a system call?
Why does kernel mode have higher privileges?

questions.content: Compare process and thread from the perspective of a backend service.
reference_points: A process has its own address space and resources
Threads share process memory but have separate stacks and execution contexts
Thread switching is usually cheaper than process switching
Shared memory makes threads efficient but increases synchronization risk
Backend servers often use processes, threads, async tasks, or a mixture
follow_up_directions: Why can threads affect each other more easily?
How does Python concurrency complicate this topic?

questions.content: What is a context switch, and why does it matter for high-concurrency backend systems?
reference_points: A context switch saves one execution context and restores another
It can happen between processes or threads
Switching has CPU overhead and can affect cache locality
Too many runnable threads can cause scheduling overhead
Async I/O and reasonable thread pools can reduce unnecessary switching
follow_up_directions: How would you detect excessive context switches?
Why can too many threads reduce throughput?

questions.content: Explain user mode, kernel mode, and system calls.
reference_points: User mode has limited privileges and runs application code
Kernel mode has privileged access to hardware and core OS resources
System calls let user programs request privileged services
Examples include file I/O, network I/O, process creation, and memory mapping
Crossing the user-kernel boundary has overhead, so batching and buffering can help performance
follow_up_directions: Why should applications minimize unnecessary system calls?
What happens during a network read system call?

questions.content: What is deadlock, and what are the necessary conditions for it?
reference_points: Deadlock means tasks wait forever for resources held by each other
Necessary conditions include mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, and circular wait
It can appear in threads, database transactions, distributed locks, and message processing
Prevention can break one of the necessary conditions
Detection requires monitoring wait graphs, timeouts, or lock diagnostics
follow_up_directions: How can lock ordering prevent deadlock?
How does database deadlock detection work?

questions.content: What is virtual memory, and why is it important?
reference_points: Virtual memory gives each process an isolated logical address space
The OS and hardware translate virtual addresses to physical addresses
It improves isolation, simplifies memory allocation, and enables demand paging
Pages can be swapped or loaded lazily from disk
Memory pressure and page faults can cause severe backend latency
follow_up_directions: What is a page fault?
Why can swapping hurt service latency?

questions.content: What is the difference between blocking I/O, non-blocking I/O, and I/O multiplexing?
reference_points: Blocking I/O waits until the operation is ready or complete
Non-blocking I/O returns immediately when data is not ready
I/O multiplexing lets one thread watch many file descriptors
select, poll, and epoll are common mechanisms on Unix-like systems
High-concurrency servers often use event loops built on multiplexing
follow_up_directions: Why is epoll efficient for many connections?
How does this relate to asyncio or Node.js?

questions.content: Explain epoll and why it is useful for high-concurrency network services.
reference_points: epoll lets applications monitor many file descriptors for readiness events
It avoids scanning all descriptors on every wait like older select-style approaches
It supports level-triggered and edge-triggered modes
It is commonly used by high-performance servers and event loops
Correct edge-triggered usage requires draining data until EAGAIN
follow_up_directions: Compare level-triggered and edge-triggered modes
What bugs can happen if edge-triggered reads are not drained?

questions.content: What is the difference between concurrency and parallelism?
reference_points: Concurrency means handling multiple tasks in overlapping time periods
Parallelism means executing multiple tasks at the same instant on multiple cores
Single-threaded async programs can be concurrent without being parallel
CPU-bound work benefits from parallelism
I/O-bound backend work often benefits from concurrency and event-driven design
follow_up_directions: Is async I/O parallel?
How would you handle CPU-bound tasks in a Python backend?

questions.content: How would you diagnose high CPU usage in a backend service?
reference_points: Check process-level and thread-level CPU usage
Use profiling to identify hot functions or tight loops
Inspect recent traffic patterns, retry storms, and background jobs
Check whether garbage collection, serialization, or compression dominates CPU
Correlate CPU usage with latency and error metrics
follow_up_directions: How would you distinguish user CPU from system CPU?
What would high system CPU indicate?

questions.content: How would you diagnose memory leaks or memory pressure in a service?
reference_points: Observe RSS, heap usage, object counts, and container memory limits
Take heap snapshots or use language-specific profilers
Check caches, global collections, unclosed resources, and large request buffers
Look for swap usage and page faults under memory pressure
Set alerts before out-of-memory kills happen
follow_up_directions: What is the difference between heap and RSS?
How can an unbounded cache become a memory leak?

questions.content: What is a file descriptor, and why can backend services run out of them?
reference_points: A file descriptor is an OS handle for files, sockets, pipes, and other I/O resources
Each open network connection usually consumes a descriptor
Leaks can occur when files or sockets are not closed
High concurrency may exceed per-process or system limits
Monitoring open descriptors and using connection pooling can prevent failures
follow_up_directions: What symptoms appear when descriptors are exhausted?
How would you inspect open descriptors on Linux?

questions.content: What is the difference between a mutex, semaphore, and condition variable?
reference_points: A mutex protects a critical section with exclusive ownership
A semaphore manages a count of available resources
A condition variable lets threads wait for a condition while releasing a lock
Misuse can cause deadlocks, missed signals, or race conditions
Backend examples include connection pools, worker queues, and shared caches
follow_up_directions: When would you use a semaphore for a connection pool?
What is a spurious wakeup?

questions.content: How do containers relate to operating system isolation?
reference_points: Containers share the host kernel but isolate processes using namespaces
cgroups limit and account for CPU, memory, and other resources
File systems are layered through image and overlay mechanisms
Containers are lighter than virtual machines but provide weaker isolation boundaries
Backend services must respect container memory and CPU limits
follow_up_directions: What happens when a container exceeds its memory limit?
Why can CPU limits affect latency?

questions.content: How would you explain event loops in backend frameworks?
reference_points: An event loop waits for I/O readiness and dispatches callbacks or coroutines
It allows many concurrent I/O operations with fewer threads
Blocking CPU work inside the event loop hurts all concurrent requests
Async frameworks still need thread or process pools for blocking operations
Correct timeout, cancellation, and backpressure handling are important
follow_up_directions: Why should database drivers be async-compatible?
How would you handle CPU-heavy work in an event loop service?

questions.content: What properties does a database transaction provide?
reference_points: Atomicity means all operations commit together or roll back together
Consistency means constraints and invariants are preserved
Isolation controls how concurrent transactions observe each other
Durability means committed data survives crashes
Backend code should understand which guarantees the database and isolation level actually provide
follow_up_directions: Which ACID property is most related to isolation levels?
How can distributed transactions complicate ACID?

questions.content: Explain common transaction isolation levels and anomalies.
reference_points: Read uncommitted can allow dirty reads
Read committed prevents dirty reads but may allow non-repeatable reads
Repeatable read prevents non-repeatable reads and often uses MVCC
Serializable provides the strongest isolation but may reduce concurrency
Anomalies include dirty read, non-repeatable read, phantom read, and write skew
follow_up_directions: What isolation level does MySQL InnoDB commonly use by default?
What is a phantom read?

questions.content: What is MVCC, and how does it improve database concurrency?
reference_points: MVCC keeps multiple versions of rows to support consistent reads
Readers can avoid blocking writers in many scenarios
Transactions read a snapshot based on visibility rules
Old versions require cleanup such as purge or vacuum mechanisms
MVCC improves read concurrency but does not remove all locking needs
follow_up_directions: How does MVCC relate to undo logs?
Can MVCC prevent all write conflicts?

questions.content: Why are database indexes useful, and what tradeoffs do they introduce?
reference_points: Indexes reduce the amount of data scanned for queries
B+ tree indexes support range queries and ordered traversal
Indexes speed up reads but add write overhead and storage cost
Too many indexes slow inserts, updates, and deletes
Index design should follow actual query patterns
follow_up_directions: Why is a B+ tree suitable for databases?
How would you identify unused indexes?

questions.content: What is the leftmost prefix rule for composite indexes?
reference_points: A composite index can be used efficiently from its leftmost columns
Skipping the leading column usually prevents full use of the index
Range conditions can limit the usefulness of later columns
Column order should match common equality, range, and sorting patterns
Explain plans should be checked rather than assuming index usage
follow_up_directions: How would you order columns in a composite index?
Why can a range condition stop further index use?

questions.content: How would you analyze a slow SQL query?
reference_points: Capture the slow query and representative parameters
Use EXPLAIN or execution plans to inspect scan type, rows, join order, and index usage
Check whether predicates, sorting, grouping, or joins miss suitable indexes
Inspect data distribution, table statistics, and returned row count
Consider query rewrite, index changes, pagination strategy, or denormalization
follow_up_directions: What does a full table scan indicate?
How can pagination become slow?

questions.content: What is the difference between clustered and secondary indexes in InnoDB?
reference_points: InnoDB stores table data in the clustered index organized by primary key
A secondary index stores indexed columns plus the primary key value
Looking up non-covered columns through a secondary index requires a back-to-table lookup
Primary key choice affects storage locality and secondary index size
A covering index can avoid extra lookups
follow_up_directions: Why should primary keys be short and stable?
What is a covering index?

questions.content: What is a covering index, and when is it useful?
reference_points: A covering index contains all columns needed by a query
The database can answer the query from the index without reading table rows
It can improve read latency for high-frequency queries
It increases index size and write cost
It is most useful for narrow query projections and stable access patterns
follow_up_directions: How can SELECT * prevent covering index benefits?
What is the cost of adding covering indexes?

questions.content: How would you design pagination for large tables?
reference_points: Offset pagination becomes slow when offset is large because many rows are skipped
Keyset pagination uses a stable cursor such as id or created_at
The cursor column should be indexed and deterministic
Sorting must be stable to avoid missing or duplicated rows
Frontend requirements determine whether jumping to arbitrary pages is needed
follow_up_directions: Why is LIMIT 100000, 20 slow?
How would you paginate by created_at with duplicate timestamps?

questions.content: What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic locking?
reference_points: Pessimistic locking assumes conflicts are likely and locks records before update
Optimistic locking assumes conflicts are rare and checks a version or timestamp during update
Pessimistic locks can block and create deadlock risk
Optimistic locking needs retry or conflict handling logic
Choice depends on contention level and business semantics
follow_up_directions: How would you implement optimistic locking in SQL?
When would pessimistic locking be safer?

questions.content: What are database deadlocks, and how should applications handle them?
reference_points: Database deadlocks happen when transactions wait on each other's locks
Databases often detect deadlocks and abort one transaction
Applications should use short transactions and consistent lock ordering
Retrying aborted transactions can be correct if operations are idempotent
Deadlock logs help identify conflicting SQL statements and lock order
follow_up_directions: Why can two simple updates deadlock?
How many times should an application retry?

questions.content: How would you design a database schema for high write throughput?
reference_points: Keep write paths narrow and avoid unnecessary indexes
Use append-friendly primary keys when appropriate
Batch writes and reduce transaction size when possible
Separate hot counters or frequently updated fields from wide rows
Consider partitioning, sharding, or asynchronous processing when a single table becomes a bottleneck
follow_up_directions: How can too many secondary indexes hurt writes?
What is a hot row problem?

questions.content: What is database normalization, and when would you denormalize?
reference_points: Normalization reduces redundancy and update anomalies
It separates entities into related tables with clear keys
Denormalization duplicates data to improve read performance or simplify queries
Denormalized data must be kept consistent by application logic or pipelines
Backend design balances correctness, query complexity, and performance
follow_up_directions: What consistency issues can denormalization introduce?
Where would denormalization help a reporting page?

questions.content: How would you choose between relational databases and NoSQL databases?
reference_points: Relational databases fit structured data, transactions, joins, and strong consistency needs
NoSQL systems can fit flexible schemas, horizontal scale, or specialized access patterns
Document, key-value, column-family, and graph databases solve different problems
Operational maturity and query requirements matter as much as data volume
Many systems use relational databases as source of truth and NoSQL for specialized workloads
follow_up_directions: Would you store orders in MongoDB or MySQL?
How would you handle transactions in NoSQL?

questions.content: What is read-write splitting, and what issues can it introduce?
reference_points: Writes go to the primary database and reads can go to replicas
It improves read scalability when replicas are available
Replication lag can cause stale reads
Some requests may need read-your-writes consistency and must hit the primary
Routing logic and failover behavior must be carefully tested
follow_up_directions: How would you handle a user reading data immediately after writing?
What happens during primary failover?

questions.content: What is sharding, and how would you choose a shard key?
reference_points: Sharding splits data across multiple database instances
A good shard key distributes load and supports common query patterns
Poor shard keys can create hot shards or expensive cross-shard queries
Rebalancing and resharding are operationally complex
Global unique ids and distributed transactions need extra design
follow_up_directions: Why is user_id often a shard key?
How would you query across shards?

questions.content: How would you design id generation in a distributed backend system?
reference_points: Options include database auto-increment, UUID, Snowflake-like ids, and segment allocation
Auto-increment is simple but can bottleneck or reveal sequence information
UUIDs are decentralized but can be large and index-unfriendly
Snowflake-style ids encode time and worker information
The choice depends on ordering, uniqueness, storage, and operational constraints
follow_up_directions: Why can random UUIDs hurt B+ tree locality?
What clock issues affect Snowflake ids?

questions.content: What is the difference between binlog, redo log, and undo log in MySQL-like systems?
reference_points: Redo log supports crash recovery for committed changes
Undo log supports rollback and MVCC visibility of older versions
Binlog records logical changes and is used for replication and point-in-time recovery
Redo log is storage-engine level, while binlog is server-level in MySQL
Two-phase commit coordinates redo log and binlog consistency
follow_up_directions: Why is two-phase commit needed between redo log and binlog?
How does undo log relate to snapshot reads?

questions.content: How would you avoid SQL injection in backend APIs?
reference_points: Use parameterized queries or ORM parameter binding
Never concatenate untrusted input into SQL strings
Validate and whitelist dynamic identifiers such as sort fields
Use least-privilege database accounts
Log suspicious patterns without leaking sensitive data
follow_up_directions: Can ORM usage still be vulnerable?
How would you safely support dynamic ORDER BY?

questions.content: How would you design database backups and recovery for an online product?
reference_points: Use full backups plus incremental logs or binlogs for point-in-time recovery
Define RPO and RTO based on business requirements
Regularly test restore procedures rather than only creating backups
Store backups in isolated and durable locations
Monitor backup success, size, and restore time
follow_up_directions: What is the difference between RPO and RTO?
Why must restore drills be automated?

questions.content: What are common Redis use cases in backend systems?
reference_points: Redis is commonly used as a distributed cache
It can support distributed locks, rate limiting, counters, leaderboards, and sessions
Redis data structures include String, Hash, List, Set, Sorted Set, Bitmap, HyperLogLog, and Stream
It is fast because data is mainly kept in memory
Using Redis requires careful expiration, consistency, and memory management
follow_up_directions: When should Redis not be used?
Which Redis data structure would you use for a leaderboard?

questions.content: How would you design a cache-aside strategy with Redis?
reference_points: Read path checks Redis first and falls back to the database on miss
After database read, the service writes the value back to Redis with TTL
Write path typically updates the database and deletes or invalidates the cache
Deleting cache after database update avoids some stale overwrite scenarios
Failures need retry, monitoring, and possibly delayed double deletion
follow_up_directions: Why delete cache instead of updating it directly?
What is delayed double deletion?

questions.content: What are cache penetration, cache breakdown, and cache avalanche?
reference_points: Cache penetration means requests for nonexistent data bypass cache and hit the database repeatedly
Cache breakdown means one hot key expires and many requests hit the database at once
Cache avalanche means many keys expire together or Redis becomes unavailable
Mitigations include null-value caching, Bloom filters, mutex rebuild, random TTL, and fallback limits
Protection should be tested under traffic spikes
follow_up_directions: How does a Bloom filter reduce penetration?
How would you rebuild a hot key safely?

questions.content: How would you implement a distributed lock with Redis?
reference_points: Use SET key value NX PX to acquire a lock with expiration
Use a unique token as the lock value to avoid releasing another client's lock
Release the lock with an atomic Lua script that checks the token before deleting
Set expiration to prevent permanent deadlock if the owner crashes
Long tasks may require renewal or a watchdog mechanism
follow_up_directions: Why is a unique lock token necessary?
What problems does Redlock try to solve?

questions.content: Compare Redis RDB and AOF persistence mechanisms.
reference_points: RDB creates point-in-time snapshots and is compact for backup
AOF logs write commands and can reduce data loss depending on fsync policy
RDB recovery can be faster but may lose recent writes
AOF can be larger and needs rewrite compaction
Production configuration balances durability, performance, and recovery needs
follow_up_directions: What is AOF rewrite?
Which persistence mode would you choose for cache-only Redis?

questions.content: Why is Redis fast even though it is mostly single-threaded for command execution?
reference_points: Redis stores data in memory and uses efficient data structures
Single-threaded command execution avoids many lock contention costs
I/O multiplexing handles many connections efficiently
Commands are usually simple and fast
Slow commands, large keys, persistence, and network overhead can still cause latency
follow_up_directions: Which Redis operations can block the server?
How would you detect slow commands?

questions.content: What Redis data structure would you choose for common backend scenarios?
reference_points: String fits simple cache values, counters, and tokens
Hash fits object-like fields such as user profiles
Set fits uniqueness and intersection operations
Sorted Set fits leaderboards and delayed task scores
Stream fits lightweight message stream use cases with consumer groups
follow_up_directions: How would you implement a leaderboard?
When would Hash be better than String?

questions.content: How would you design rate limiting with Redis?
reference_points: Fixed window counters are simple but can allow boundary bursts
Sliding window strategies smooth traffic at higher cost
Token bucket and leaky bucket control burst and steady rates differently
Lua scripts make multi-step checks and updates atomic
Limits can be per user, IP, API key, route, or session
follow_up_directions: What is the weakness of fixed window limiting?
Why use Lua for rate limiting?

questions.content: How would you handle Redis memory pressure and eviction?
reference_points: Set maxmemory according to the deployment budget
Choose an eviction policy such as allkeys-lru, volatile-lru, allkeys-lfu, or noeviction
Use TTLs for cache keys and avoid unbounded key growth
Monitor memory fragmentation, big keys, hot keys, and eviction count
Compress or split large values when appropriate
follow_up_directions: What happens under noeviction policy?
How would you find big keys?

questions.content: What are hot keys and big keys in Redis, and why are they dangerous?
reference_points: A hot key receives disproportionately high traffic and can overload a node
A big key stores too much data and can block operations or slow network transfer
Hot keys can be mitigated with local cache, key splitting, or request coalescing
Big keys should be split, paginated, or modeled with smaller structures
Detection requires metrics, command stats, latency monitoring, and key scans in safe ways
follow_up_directions: How would you split a hot leaderboard key?
Why is KEYS dangerous in production?

questions.content: How does Redis Cluster distribute data?
reference_points: Redis Cluster divides keyspace into hash slots
Each master node owns a subset of slots
Keys are mapped to slots using CRC16 and optional hash tags
Clients must handle MOVED or ASK redirections during topology changes
Cluster improves horizontal scaling but adds multi-key operation constraints
follow_up_directions: What are hash tags in Redis Cluster?
Why are cross-slot multi-key operations limited?

questions.content: How would you use Redis Streams, and how do they compare with professional message queues?
reference_points: Redis Streams provide append-only messages with ids and consumer groups
They support pending entries and acknowledgement
They can be useful for lightweight internal streams
Professional MQ systems usually provide stronger tooling, durability, throughput, and ecosystem support
Redis Streams should be chosen only when their operational tradeoffs are acceptable
follow_up_directions: What is a pending entry list?
Why might Kafka or RocketMQ be better for critical event pipelines?

questions.content: How would you implement a delayed task queue with Redis?
reference_points: Use a Sorted Set where the score is the execution timestamp
Workers poll or block around due timestamps and claim tasks atomically
Lua scripts can atomically fetch and remove due tasks
Task execution must be idempotent because retries can occur
For high reliability or large scale, a dedicated MQ delay feature may be better
follow_up_directions: How would you prevent two workers from executing the same task?
When would you choose RocketMQ delayed messages instead?

questions.content: What consistency issues can appear when using Redis as a cache in front of MySQL?
reference_points: Database updates and cache updates are not naturally atomic
Updating cache before database commit can expose uncommitted or wrong data
Deleting cache after database update is common but still has race windows
Delayed deletion, message-based invalidation, and short TTL can reduce inconsistency
Strong consistency may require reading from the database or using transactional patterns
follow_up_directions: What race can happen between cache miss and database update?
How would you choose TTL for critical data?

questions.content: How would you monitor Redis in production?
reference_points: Track latency, QPS, memory, connected clients, evictions, and blocked clients
Monitor hit rate, key count, expired keys, and slow log
Watch replication lag and cluster slot health
Alert on memory nearing maxmemory and increasing command latency
Correlate Redis metrics with application latency and database load
follow_up_directions: What does a falling cache hit rate indicate?
How would you investigate sudden Redis latency spikes?

questions.content: Why do backend systems use message queues?
reference_points: Message queues decouple producers and consumers
They support asynchronous processing to reduce request latency
They absorb traffic spikes and smooth downstream load
They improve extensibility when multiple downstream systems subscribe to events
They introduce complexity such as eventual consistency, duplicates, and delayed failure handling
follow_up_directions: When would you avoid using MQ?
Give an example of asynchronous processing in an order system

questions.content: How would you prevent message loss in a message queue pipeline?
reference_points: Producer should use reliable send acknowledgements and retry carefully
Broker should persist messages and replicate data when needed
Consumer should acknowledge only after successful processing
Dead-letter queues help isolate repeatedly failing messages
Monitoring should cover send failures, broker health, consumer lag, and retry counts
follow_up_directions: Where can a message be lost?
How would you handle producer retry duplicates?

questions.content: Why can messages be consumed more than once, and how would you design idempotent consumers?
reference_points: Retries, timeouts, consumer crashes, and acknowledgement failures can cause duplicate delivery
Consumers should use business ids or message ids to deduplicate
Database unique constraints can enforce idempotency
Side effects should be designed so repeated execution is safe
Exactly-once semantics often require careful end-to-end design rather than broker configuration alone
follow_up_directions: What idempotency key would you use for a payment callback?
Can exactly-once delivery be guaranteed easily?

questions.content: How would you handle message backlog or consumer lag?
reference_points: Identify whether backlog comes from traffic spike, slow consumer logic, downstream bottleneck, or broker issue
Scale consumers up to the number of useful partitions or queues
Optimize slow processing and batch where safe
Temporarily route non-critical work to lower priority or delayed processing
Track lag, processing latency, retry rate, and dead-letter growth
follow_up_directions: Why might adding consumers not improve throughput?
How would you recover from millions of delayed messages?

questions.content: How would you guarantee message ordering when using MQ?
reference_points: Global ordering reduces concurrency and is rarely necessary
Per-key ordering is usually more practical, such as ordering by order_id or user_id
Send messages for the same key to the same partition or queue
Consume each ordered partition or queue sequentially
Ordering must be balanced against throughput and failure recovery
follow_up_directions: Why is global ordering expensive?
How would you guarantee order events are processed in order?

questions.content: Compare Kafka, RocketMQ, and RabbitMQ for backend use cases.
reference_points: Kafka is strong for high-throughput logs, event streams, and analytics pipelines
RocketMQ is often used for online business messaging, delayed messages, and transactional messages
RabbitMQ is feature-rich for routing patterns and traditional messaging
Selection depends on throughput, latency, ordering, delayed messages, transactions, ecosystem, and team experience
Operational maturity and failure handling matter more than benchmark numbers alone
follow_up_directions: When would Kafka be a poor fit?
Why might RocketMQ be chosen for e-commerce order events?

questions.content: How do Kafka partitions and consumer groups work?
reference_points: A topic is divided into partitions for parallelism and ordering within each partition
Messages with the same key can be routed to the same partition
Consumers in the same group share partitions for load balancing
Different consumer groups each receive their own copy of the topic stream
Consumer lag measures how far consumers are behind partition offsets
follow_up_directions: Why does partition count limit consumer parallelism within one group?
What happens during consumer rebalance?

questions.content: How would you design transactional messaging between a database update and an event publish?
reference_points: The core problem is atomicity between local database changes and message publishing
The outbox pattern writes business data and event records in the same database transaction
A relay later publishes outbox events to the message queue
Consumers still need idempotency because messages may be published more than once
Some MQ systems provide transactional messages, but business reconciliation is still important
follow_up_directions: Why is dual-write unsafe?
How would you ensure outbox events are eventually published?

questions.content: What is a dead-letter queue, and when should it be used?
reference_points: A dead-letter queue stores messages that cannot be processed successfully after retries
It prevents poison messages from blocking normal consumption
DLQ messages should include error metadata and original payload references
Teams should monitor and replay DLQ messages after fixing the root cause
A DLQ is not a substitute for idempotency or validation
follow_up_directions: How would you replay DLQ messages safely?
What is a poison message?

questions.content: How would you design delayed or scheduled messages?
reference_points: Use native delay features when the chosen MQ supports the required precision and scale
Use Redis Sorted Set or a database scheduler for simpler systems
Delayed tasks must be idempotent because retries and duplicate delivery can occur
Very large delayed queues need monitoring and backpressure
Use cases include order timeout, retry delay, reminder notification, and compensation tasks
follow_up_directions: How would you implement order timeout cancellation?
When would a simple database scheduler be enough?
